title: The Rise of the NSDAP, 1919–1933
subtitle: From Beer Hall to the Chancellery
author: VeteransLedger Archive
summary: The National Socialist German Workers' Party rose from a minor Munich extremist group in 1919 to seize power in Germany by January 1933, exploiting the economic and political crises of the Weimar Republic.

body.text: The NSDAP's rise to power is one of the most studied political phenomena of the twentieth century — a cautionary case study in how democratic institutions can be dismantled from within. Between 1919 and 1933, a fringe movement of nationalist extremists transformed into the governing party of Europe's most industrialised nation.

The Deutsche Arbeiterpartei (DAP) was founded in Munich on 5 January 1919 by Anton Drexler, a railway locksmith, and Karl Harrer, a journalist. Hitler attended his first meeting in September 1919 as an army intelligence agent sent to monitor the group. Instead, he joined. By 1921 he had replaced Drexler as leader, renamed the party the NSDAP, and introduced the Sturmabteilung (SA) as its paramilitary wing.

Emboldened by Mussolini's successful March on Rome in 1922, Hitler attempted to seize power in Munich on 8–9 November 1923. The putsch failed; Hitler was arrested and sentenced to five years in prison (he served nine months). During his imprisonment at Landsberg am Lech, he dictated the first volume of Mein Kampf to Rudolf Hess.

The failed putsch led Hitler to conclude that power must be achieved through the 'legal path' — through elections and parliamentary manipulation rather than revolution. This strategic pivot proved crucial to the party's eventual success.

The Wall Street Crash of October 1929 triggered a global depression that devastated Germany. By 1932, unemployment reached 6 million — approximately 30% of the workforce. The Weimar Republic's political system was paralysed, with governments relying on emergency decrees under Article 48 of the constitution.

In this environment, the NSDAP's electoral support exploded: from 2.6% in May 1928 to 18.3% in September 1930 to 37.4% in July 1932, making it the largest party in the Reichstag. The party's mass rallies, propaganda machine under Joseph Goebbels, and promise of national renewal attracted voters across the political spectrum who had lost faith in Weimar democracy.

NSDAP support had actually begun to decline by November 1932, dropping to 33.1% in the elections of that month. However, political manoeuvring by Franz von Papen and other conservative politicians convinced President Hindenburg to appoint Hitler Chancellor on 30 January 1933. They believed they could control him within a conservative cabinet. They were catastrophically wrong.

Within two months, the Reichstag Fire Decree suspended civil liberties, mass arrests of political opponents began, and the Enabling Act transferred legislative power to Hitler's cabinet. The Weimar Republic was over. Germany had become a dictatorship.
